_comment: [34.6] / [59.3] THE INITIAL AIR STRENGTHS -- the aeroplanes each side actually has, by type, at the start of the game. Until this file existed the whole Axis strike establishment was a ~4-plane proxy (game.air's six representative types plus scenario._AXIS_AIR_STRIKE: three Ju. 87B before Game-Turn 35 and one after), which is why percentages of it rounded to zero and 84 of 111 Malta raids delivered no bomb points at all. [60.32] musters 133 S.M. 79 Sparvieros ALONE. Every number below is transcribed from the 1979 book -- the muster rows read WITH EYES off the scan at 300 dpi (PDF page 78, the whole of [60.32] and [60.42] in one column each) because the OCR in docs/rules/60 drops the Morane 406's Available figure. Each row's `type` is the name the [4.44A/b/c] Aircraft Characteristics Chart PRINTS (data/logistics_rates.json aircraft_characteristics_4_44), periods and all, because game.roster and game.basing match on those keys EXACTLY and a prose-spelled list already failed silently once (the 43.11 'He 111' / 'FW 220' bug). Where the scenario spells the name differently, its spelling is carried beside it as `printed`.
_rule_59_3: [59.31] verbatim: 'Each scenario lists the starting Axis and Allied plane types, numbers, pilots, air facilities, and squadron ground support units. This listing is divided into forces available in NORTH AFRICA AND MALTA for the Allied Player and to forces available in NORTH AFRICA AND SICILY/ITALY/CRETE for the Axis Player.' [59.32] verbatim: 'Planes are listed by type, WITH THE READY PLANES INDICATED AS A PORTION OF THE TOTAL AVAILABLE. All planes (refitted or not) are considered to begin a scenario fueled and armed (at no cost to the supplies available).' So the muster's second column is 38.31's readiness ledger at Game-Turn 1 and it OVERRIDES 38.31's own 'at the start of a Scenario, all planes are considered refitted' -- the scenario is the more specific rule, and it is what game.scenario seeds GameState.air_unfit from. [59.33] leaves the distribution among squadrons to the owner ('within the restrictions on squadron composition listed in Case 35.2'); [59.36] 'Maintenance may not be performed on planes during the first OpStage of a Scenario' -- BUILT 2026-07-22, together with [60.32]'s Axis half of the same window ('The Italian Player may not attempt to refit planes until Game-Turn 1, OpStage 2'), as one gate at the top of engine._air_maintenance. It was flagged unbuilt when this file first shipped and that was the wrong disposition: seeding air_unfit from the Refitted column is precisely what made those two sentences bite for the first time, because before it nothing was ever undergoing refit in the first stage and the bar cost nothing.
_role_assignment: THE ROLE COLUMN IS OURS, NOT THE BOOK'S, AND IT IS THE ONE JUDGEMENT IN THIS FILE. game.state.AirWing carries Air Points in three buckets -- fighters (34.13 TacAir, the 40/45/46 superiority contest), strike (34.14 Bombload, the 41.3 bombing missions) and recon (42.2's fog-lift) -- and an aeroplane must be in exactly ONE of them, because putting a type in two would double-count it exactly as the 5.5 repair pass found rule 43 double-counting the Sicilian contingent. The book does not print a three-way partition; what it prints is the Mission Capability block, and every assignment below is read off that block plus 34.2/34.3/34.4/34.5's class definitions: a type on the FIGHTERS & FIGHTER-BOMBERS chart takes `fighters` (34.21 -- they fly CAP, and they are the only planes that need a pilot); a bomber with a Bombload and a D or B cell takes `strike`; a type whose printed capability is R takes `recon`. WHERE A TYPE CARRIES BOTH R AND B the assignment is a reading and is called out on the row: the Ca 309 Ghibli and the Ro 37bis are the Regia Aeronautica's army-cooperation aeroplanes (four and two Bomb Points respectively, against the S.M. 79's twelve) and are fielded as RECON; the S.M. 81 Pipistrello goes the other way on the same evidence -- eleven Bomb Points and the chart's only Italian NIGHT bombing cell -- and is fielded as STRIKE. The Sunderland is a THIRD case and not one of these: its two printed rows are 34.12 READYINGS, not one row with two capability cells, and the readying applied is the first printed one (238 hexes, R, no bombload), so it is fielded as RECON -- see its own _role note. (Corrected 2026-07-22: this paragraph gave the Ghibli's and the Ro 37bis's Bomb Points in the wrong order and listed the Sunderland as going with the S.M. 81, which contradicted both the row below and the data. A reader taking it at face value would have believed eighteen Sunderlands stood in the Commonwealth strike pool at Bombload 9; the Commonwealth strike arm is 56 planes and 382 Bomb Points, and always was.) tests/test_establishment.py asserts every row's role against the printed cells, so a role that contradicts the chart fails loudly.
_what_is_not_here: THE ESTABLISHMENT DOES NOT GROW, AND THAT IS A TRANSCRIPTION GAP, NOT A RULE. [34.84] 'Airplane reinforcements arrive in the Naval Convoy Arrival Phase... listed as to the total of each type of plane arriving during that month', off [34.86] (the Commonwealth Airplane Reinforcement and Squadron Withdrawal Schedule) and [34.87] (the Axis Airplane Reinforcement Schedule) -- NEITHER OF WHICH IS TRANSCRIBED, in this file or in docs/rules/90-charts, which carries only the [34.86] PILOT table under that number. THE LARGEST SINGLE CONSEQUENCE: there is no German aeroplane in this file at all, because there is none in [60.32] -- the September-1940 Axis air force in Africa is the Regia Aeronautica entire, and the Luftwaffe arrives on [34.87]'s schedule. So rule 43's TYPED cases (43.11/43.13, which name He. 111s, Ju. 88Ds and FW220s) bind on nothing in the campaign until that schedule lands, and 43.12's untyped 'all German bombers' sentence has no German bomber to base. The same gap holds Malta at its printed 31 aeroplanes for the whole war (34.81A/34.86) and is written up at data/malta_44.json later_scenario_establishments. ALSO NOT HERE: the musters of the other four scenario groups -- [61.42] (Desert Fox: five Italian types printed, plus 'all of the German planes listed in the Axis Airplane Reinforcement Chart', which is the same untranscribed [34.87]), [61.33], [62.33]/[62.43] and [63.33]/[63.43]. The campaign takes section 60's set-up by [64.3], so section 60 is what the campaign needs and what is transcribed.

campaign_64._provenance: [64.3] verbatim: 'If the entire campaign is being played, the Players use the information provided in SECTION 60.0 for the initial placement and distribution of their land, sea, and air forces, supplies and construction.' So the campaign's Initial Air Strengths ARE [60.32] and [60.42], and Malta's are [60.46] (transcribed separately, in data/malta_44.json, because rule 44 makes the island its own place with its own ledger).

AXIS._rule: [60.32] ITALIAN AIR STRENGTHS, verbatim: 'The following planes may be placed at any Italian airfields, landing strips, flying boat basins, etc., within the capacities of those facilities. However, NO PLANES START THE GAME IN ITALY/SICILY. Crete is unavailable (it is still British).' And below the table: 'The Italian Player may not attempt to refit planes until Game-Turn 1, OpStage 2.'
AXIS._theatre: THE WHOLE AXIS AIR FORCE STARTS IN AFRICA, AND STARTS IS THE LOAD-BEARING WORD. That printed sentence looked for two blocks like a contradiction of rule 44 -- [44.21]/[44.25]/[44.27] make planes BASED IN ITALY/SICILY the precondition for any raid on Malta at all, and [60.32] puts none there -- and it was carried as an open owner ruling with a percentage standing in for the answer. Ruled 2026-07-22 (Eve): [60.32] is a PLACEMENT rule with two exclusions, so the sentence is a fact about GAME-TURN 1 and nothing more. The Axis flies his bombers to Sicily when he wants them there, on a [42.1] transfer mission out of Benghazi or Derna, and the desert goes without them while they are gone. See italy_sicily_basing_43_1.transfer_42_1 in data/malta_44.json and game/basing.py.

planes._role: R and B both printed (D - / R ! / B N). Fielded as STRIKE: eleven Bomb Points and a NIGHT bombing capability -- the Pipistrello is the chart's heaviest Italian night bomber, and its R cell is the transport/liaison role its 1P-or-2.5-tons Transport cell belongs to.

R and B both printed (D - / R ! / B !). Fielded as RECON: four Bomb Points against the S.M. 79's twelve, Maneuver 9, and the Ghibli is the Regia Aeronautica's colonial army-cooperation aeroplane. This is a reading; the cells are transcribed so it can be re-read.

R and B both printed. Fielded as RECON on the same evidence: two Bomb Points. The IMAM Ro.37bis is the Italian army co-operation type, the exact counterpart of the Luftwaffe's Hs. 126 which this engine already flies as recon.

planes._type: OWNER RULING MADE 2026-07-22 (Eve): [60.32]'s NINTH ROW, PRINTED '2S01', IS THE CANT Z. 501 GABBIANO. The row is real -- PDF page 78, second column, rendered at 300 dpi and read with eyes twice: the glyphs are digit-two, capital-S, digit-zero, digit-one, and the row is '2S01 | 9 | 9'. The rule's own sentence is what settles it: [60.32] licenses placement 'at any Italian airfields, landing strips, FLYING BOAT BASINS, etc.', and the [4.44b] Italian Aircraft Characteristics Chart (PDF p.144, rendered and read for this block) prints exactly one Italian flying boat the Regia Aeronautica could have based in Libya in September 1940 -- 'Cant Z. 501 Gabbiano', Range 130, TacAir (2), Maneuver 7, Bomb 7, Fuel 2, D - / R ! / B !. A 'Z.501' whose Z sets as a 2 and whose 5 sets as an S is '2S01'. Same class as 43.11's 'FW220' and ruled the same way, by the owner and against a transcribed chart row rather than by us against a guess. The nine aeroplanes are now IN the establishment; the muster totals 394, which is the number the page adds up to.
planes._role: R and B both printed, exactly as the Ca 309 and the Ro 37bis above (D - / R ! / B !), and fielded as RECON on the same reading plus one more: [34.4]/[4.44b Notes] make the Gabbiano a FLYING BOAT ('the German Ar. 196 and Italian Cant Z.506B Airone and Cant Z.501 Gabbiano are flying boats'), and a maritime patrol flying boat confined to a basin or an alighting area is the Regia Aeronautica's naval reconnaissance arm. It is the exact counterpart of the Commonwealth's eighteen Sunderlands, which this file already fields as recon on the same argument. Seven Bomb Points against the S.M. 79's twelve is the one cell that points the other way, and it is transcribed so the reading can be re-read.

pilots_60_32._rule: [60.32] 'Italian Pilots Available: Three's: 3. Two's: 10. One's: 15.' [34.23] every fighter and fighter-bomber is assigned a pilot, and [40.1] pilots modify air-to-air combat. NOTHING READS THIS: rule 40 is deferred (game.basing's debt block -- nothing on the African mainland can be shot down), and until it lands a pilot rating has nothing to modify. Transcribed because the book prints it and because 34.83's Pilot Arrival Tables are the schedule it grows on.

sgsus_60_32._rule: [60.32] 'Italian SGSU Available: 39.' Already read by game.oob (logistics_data.italian_sgsu_available_60_32) and repeated here so the muster is whole. [35.23] gives an Italian Squadriglia 12 planes, so 39 SGSUs can work 468 aeroplanes -- comfortably more than the 394 mustered, which is the cross-check that the two rows belong together.

ALLIED._rule: [60.42] COMMONWEALTH NORTH AFRICAN AIR FORCE, verbatim: 'The following planes, SGSU's and pilots may be placed at any Commonwealth N. African air facility, within the capacity of that facility.' The Malta establishment is listed separately at [60.46] and is transcribed in data/malta_44.json -- [59.31] divides the Commonwealth's forces into 'North Africa and Malta' and this is the North African half.

planes._role: The chart's operational readying (34.12) carries Bomb 10 and a B cell; its other row is the '**' transfer configuration and has no mission capability at all. Fielded as STRIKE on the row the book gives it a bombload on.

Two operational readyings (34.12) and the choice is the owner's: 238 hexes with an R cell, or 176 hexes with 9 Bomb Points and a B cell. Fielded as RECON on the FIRST printed row -- the Sunderland is a flying boat (the chart's own Notes) confined by 34.4 to the basin/alighting facilities, and long-ranged naval reconnaissance is what an eighteen-aeroplane Sunderland force in the Mediterranean is for.

The IVF is on the FIGHTERS & FIGHTER-BOMBERS chart (PDF p.112), TacAir 5, no bombload -- the long-range fighter conversion of the same airframe as the two bombers above it. Its Scramble cell is the chart's '0': 'The Blenheim IVF may be assigned only Night Scramble missions.'

planes._available: THE OCR IN docs/rules/60 LEAVES THIS AVAILABLE FIGURE BLANK. The scan (PDF p.78, right column) prints 4 and 2, read with eyes. '*May be in the same squadron (Free French).'

planes._type: The [4.44A] chart prints this row's Aircraft name as '63.11' with Potez in the Manufacturer column; the key of the characteristics chart is therefore '63.11'.

planes._role: Bomb 10 and a B cell, printed. Fielded as STRIKE on the chart as printed, even though the Valentia is a lumbering biplane transport with TacAir 0 and Maneuver 1 -- transcribe, do not judge. Its Transport cell is '1/4 or 5 tons'.

pilots_60_42._rule: [60.42] 'Pilots Available: 1 Three, 5 Two's and 8 One's.' Unread, for the same reason as the Axis row: rule 40 is deferred.

sgsus_60_42._rule: [60.42] 'SGSU Available: 14.' Already read by game.oob (logistics_data.cw_sgsu_available_60_42). [35.23], as the owner ruled it on 2026-07-21, gives a Commonwealth squadron 20 planes until June 1941, so 14 SGSUs can work 280 aeroplanes against the 143 mustered.
